paper.title: The Disappearance of Rituals: A Topology of the Present
paper.abstract_preview: The first time I bought something at the Japanese clothing retailer Uniqlo, I casually handed the cashier my credit card, barely grasped in one hand, while I riffled through my bag with the other. He ...

concepts.name: topology of the present
concepts.definition: A spatial-analytical approach to understanding contemporary cultural and social formations, particularly around ritual practices

concepts.name: disappearance of rituals
concepts.definition: The observed decline or transformation of traditional ritual practices in contemporary society

concepts.name: liturgy of emptiness
concepts.definition: Han's concept describing formalistic practices that overwhelm meaning through excessive signification

concepts.name: overabundance of the signifier
concepts.definition: A condition where formal elements (signifiers) overwhelm and displace the search for underlying meaning (signified)

concepts.name: commodified ritual
concepts.definition: The transformation of ritual practices into commercial experiences that maintain formal elements while serving capitalist consumption

relationships.explanation: Reklis directly challenges Han's binary opposition between ritual and commodity, showing how they can coexist in capitalist contexts
Uses Barthesian concepts of signifier/signified to analyze contemporary ritual practices
Links contemporary commodification of ritual to historical patterns of colonial appropriation and romanticization
Applies spatial and temporal analysis to contemporary cultural phenomena

claims.claim: Contemporary capitalism does not eliminate ritual but transforms it into commodified experiences that maintain formal elements while serving commercial purposes
The assumption that ritual is 'already always lost' obscures how longing for ritual itself becomes a mechanism of capitalist exploitation
Han's theory of ritual as opposed to commodity fails to account for how ritual and commerce intersect in contemporary consumer culture
Nostalgia for ritual has historical roots in European colonial enterprises and continues to operate in global consumer capitalism

historical_significance.controversy_generated: Challenges popular critical theory assumptions about the incompatibility of authentic ritual and capitalist commodity relations
historical_significance.lasting_impact: Provides framework for understanding how traditional practices persist and transform within contemporary consumer capitalism rather than simply disappearing

paper.title: Decolonial research methodology: an assessment of the challenge to established practice
paper.abstract_preview: The decolonization project challenges the hegemony of Western knowledge and colonial ways of thinking. This article explores this challenge specifically in relation to research methodology. It notes h...

concepts.name: decolonial research methodology
concepts.definition: Research approaches that challenge Western knowledge hegemony and protect interests of indigenous and minority groups through alternative methodological practices

concepts.name: decolonization project
concepts.definition: Academic and social movement that challenges the hegemony of Western knowledge and colonial ways of thinking

concepts.name: positivistic/modernist/universalist approaches
concepts.definition: Traditional Western research methodologies characterized by objectivity claims and universal applicability assumptions

concepts.name: transformational paradigm
concepts.definition: Research paradigm that emphasizes social change and empowerment of marginalized communities through research practice

relationships.explanation: Decolonial methodology explicitly challenges and opposes traditional Western positivistic approaches
Alternative decolonial approaches are generally based on constructivist principles
Decolonial approaches typically involve qualitative methods such as participatory action research
Decolonial methodology operates within and extends transformational paradigm principles

claims.claim: Decolonization exposes inappropriate use of positivistic/modernist/universalist approaches by Western researchers
Alternative decolonial approaches better protect the interests of indigenous and 'Other' minority groups
Decolonial research methodology represents a distinct challenge to Western research methodology, particularly in research involving Indigenous communities
Decolonial approaches are generally based on constructivist principles and involve qualitative methods

historical_significance.controversy_generated: Assessment of established Western research practices and their appropriateness for diverse populations
historical_significance.lasting_impact: Contributes to ongoing methodological debates about culturally appropriate research practices and the need for alternative approaches in cross-cultural research contexts

paper.title: When decolonization is hijacked
paper.abstract_preview: Abstract This article asks how we should reconceptualize decolonization when it is hijacked by authoritarian/fascist forces. It focuses on the notorious Bhima Koregaon case in India in which 16 intell...

concepts.name: hijacked decolonization
concepts.definition: The appropriation of decolonization discourse by authoritarian/fascist forces to legitimize oppression rather than liberation

concepts.name: resymbolization of colonial artifacts
concepts.definition: The process by which colonized peoples reinterpret colonial symbols and monuments to resist oppression by native elites

concepts.name: state and global capital nexus
concepts.definition: The interconnected system of state power and global capitalism that creates cultural, psychological, political, and economic domination

concepts.name: street-based anticolonial intellectualism
concepts.definition: The concept that the most important anticolonial thinkers operate outside universities, in social movements and activism rather than academic institutions

concepts.name: differentiated indigenous communities
concepts.definition: A framework rejecting unitary concepts of indigenous ontology in favor of recognizing internal stratification and elite co-optation within marginalized communities

relationships.explanation: Shows how decolonization discourse can be perverted to serve oppressive rather than liberatory ends
Expands postcolonial analysis to include contemporary forms of domination beyond traditional colonialism
Challenges university-centered approaches to decolonization by locating real anticolonial work in grassroots movements
Rejects unified concepts of indigenous worldviews in favor of recognizing internal hierarchies and elite capture

claims.claim: Decolonization discourse can be hijacked by authoritarian regimes to legitimize oppression, as demonstrated in the Hindu nationalist appropriation in the Bhima Koregaon case
The most important anticolonial intellectuals operate outside universities, in social movements and often face imprisonment
Contemporary decolonization must center the state-global capital nexus rather than focusing solely on historical colonialism
Indigenous and marginalized communities contain internal hierarchies and elite co-optation that must be acknowledged rather than romanticized

historical_significance.controversy_generated: Challenges both mainstream decolonization discourse and essentialist approaches to indigenous studies, potentially controversial for questioning romanticized views of indigenous unity
historical_significance.lasting_impact: Provides critical framework for analyzing contemporary appropriations of decolonial discourse and emphasizes need for more nuanced understanding of power dynamics within marginalized communities

paper.title: Decolonising Research for Justice: Ethical Imperatives and Practical Applications
paper.abstract_preview: This article examines coloniality of research and discusses the theoretical foundations, ethical imperatives, and practical ways for conducting decolonial research for justice. It emphasises the need ...

concepts.name: coloniality of research
concepts.definition: The persistence of colonial power structures and Western dominance within research paradigms and knowledge production systems

concepts.name: decolonial research
concepts.definition: Research approaches that challenge Western epistemological dominance and center Indigenous and marginalized knowledge systems

concepts.name: transformative decolonial praxis
concepts.definition: Practical implementation of decolonial principles through action-oriented research that challenges colonial structures

concepts.name: disobedient and defiant research
concepts.definition: Research practices that deliberately resist and challenge dominant Western research paradigms and methodologies

concepts.name: epistemic justice
concepts.definition: The fair recognition and inclusion of diverse ways of knowing and knowledge systems, particularly those historically marginalized

relationships.explanation: Decolonial research practices work towards achieving epistemic justice by centering marginalized knowledge systems
The author draws on existing scholarship on disobedient research to develop transformative decolonial praxis
Decolonial research emerges as a direct response to addressing the coloniality embedded in traditional research practices

claims.claim: Western knowledge systems maintain hegemony and marginalize other epistemological traditions in research
Decolonial research requires defiance against non-relational, hierarchical, and extractive practices
Research paradigms perpetuate coloniality and need to be fundamentally reimagined
Centering non-Western voices and perspectives is essential for anti-oppressive research practice

historical_significance.controversy_generated: moderate - challenges established Western research paradigms
historical_significance.lasting_impact: Contributes to growing movement toward decolonizing academia and research practices, providing practical frameworks for implementation

paper.title: Transculturality and the Eco-Logic of Memory
paper.abstract_preview: Abstract This article asks how memory studies might use the conceptual insights gained during its “third transcultural phase” for the challenges of its “fourth phase” – heralded in this Special Issue ...

concepts.name: eco-logical memory
concepts.definition: A conceptual framework that views memory as fundamentally ecological, incorporating more-than-human elements into collective memory studies

concepts.name: transcultural memory
concepts.definition: An approach to memory studies that examines memory processes across cultural boundaries and their relational dynamics

concepts.name: relationality of the remembered
concepts.definition: A conceptual building block for eco-logical mnemohistories focusing on the relational aspects of what is remembered

concepts.name: relational remembering
concepts.definition: A conceptual building block examining the relational processes involved in acts of remembering

concepts.name: mnemonic relationality
concepts.definition: A conceptual framework exploring the inherently relational nature of memory itself

concepts.name: eco-logical mnemohistories
concepts.definition: A methodological approach to studying memory that incorporates ecological and environmental dimensions

concepts.name: fourth phase of memory studies
concepts.definition: An emerging phase of memory studies research focused on questions about memory and the environment

relationships.explanation: The work argues that relational dynamics from transcultural memory research can enable understanding of eco-logical memory
The fourth phase uses conceptual insights from the third transcultural phase to address environmental challenges
The work traces transcultural studies' roots in research on colonialism and postcolonialism
Transcultural studies emerged from research on cultural globalisation processes
The three relational concepts serve as conceptual building blocks for eco-logical mnemohistories

claims.claim: Memory is fundamentally eco-logical
Relational dynamics from transcultural memory research can enable understanding of the more-than-human in collective memory
Memory studies experienced a transcultural turn around 2010
Memory studies is entering a fourth phase focused on environmental questions

historical_significance.controversy_generated: moderate - challenges anthropocentric approaches to memory studies
historical_significance.lasting_impact: potentially significant as it proposes a new eco-logical framework that could reshape how memory studies approaches environmental questions and more-than-human agency

paper.title: Decolonizing biodiversity conservation
paper.authors: Esteve Corbera, Sara Maestre‐Andrés, Yolanda Ariadne Collins, Mathew Bukhi Mabele, Dan Brockington
paper.abstract_preview: Decolonizing biodiversity conservation science and practice involves a transition towards more locally rooted, plural, socially just, and convivial forms of conservation, moving away from mainstream c...

concepts.name: Decolonizing biodiversity conservation
concepts.definition: A transition towards more locally rooted, plural, socially just, and convivial forms of conservation, moving away from mainstream conservation approaches rooted in Eurocentric ontologies and epistemologies

concepts.name: Recognition principle
concepts.definition: A foundational principle for decolonizing conservation that acknowledges Indigenous and local knowledge systems and rights

concepts.name: Reparation principle
concepts.definition: A principle addressing historical injustices and seeking to repair damages caused by colonial conservation practices

concepts.name: Epistemic disobedience
concepts.definition: Resistance to dominant Western knowledge systems and validation of alternative ways of knowing in conservation

concepts.name: Relationality principle
concepts.definition: Emphasis on interconnected relationships between humans, nature, and spiritual dimensions in conservation practice

concepts.name: Power subversion
concepts.definition: Challenging and redistributing power structures within conservation institutions and practices

concepts.name: Limits principle
concepts.definition: Recognition of boundaries and constraints in decolonizing efforts within conservation

relationships.explanation: The framework explicitly moves away from mainstream protected area approaches as Eurocentric
Challenges market-based approaches as rooted in Eurocentric epistemologies
The concept draws from broader decolonial theoretical frameworks challenging Western knowledge dominance
Builds on established frameworks for recognizing Indigenous knowledge and rights
Extends environmental justice concerns into biodiversity conservation practice

claims.claim: Mainstream conservation approaches are strongly rooted in Eurocentric ontologies and epistemologies
Six principles (recognition, reparation, epistemic disobedience, relationality, power subversion, and limits) can serve as starting points for decolonizing conservation
claims.evidence_type: systematic review of special issue contributions

claims.claim: Implementation of decolonizing principles can result in subtler and less universalizing conservation approaches
There can be differences over meaning and emphasis regarding decolonizing principles among different stakeholders

historical_significance.controversy_generated: Challenges established conservation institutions and Western-dominated approaches, potentially creating resistance from traditional conservation organizations
historical_significance.lasting_impact: Contributes to growing movement to decolonize environmental sciences and provides framework for more inclusive and socially just conservation practices

paper.title: Ethical futures in biological anthropology: Research, teaching, community engagement, and curation involving deceased individuals
paper.authors: Carlina de la Cova, Courtney A. Hofman, Kathryn E. Marklein, Sabrina B. Sholts, Rachel Watkins, et al. (+2)
paper.abstract_preview: Although ethical reforms in biological anthropology have gained ground in recent years, there is still a scarcity of ethical standards for work involving historical documented collections (HDCs) at US...

concepts.name: Historical Documented Collections (HDCs)
concepts.definition: Collections of deceased individuals created in late 19th to mid-20th centuries under anatomy laws targeting socially marginalized communities, containing extensive documentation used for biological anthropology research

concepts.name: Ethical standards for HDC research
concepts.definition: Guidelines for ethical conduct in research, teaching, community engagement, and curation involving historical documented collections of human remains

concepts.name: Anatomy laws targeting marginalized communities
concepts.definition: Legal frameworks from late 19th to mid-20th centuries that allowed dissection of individuals from socially marginalized communities without their consent

concepts.name: Multi-stakeholder ethical workshop approach
concepts.definition: Collaborative workshop methodology bringing together researchers, curators, and community members to develop ethical consensus for biological anthropology practices

relationships.explanation: This work extends beyond NAGPRA to address collections not covered by existing repatriation laws
Applies participatory principles to develop ethical guidelines through community engagement
Directly responds to problematic historical practices in biological anthropology that lacked ethical oversight

claims.claim: There is still a scarcity of ethical standards for work involving historical documented collections at US museums and universities
HDCs were created under anatomy laws that targeted socially marginalized communities and allowed dissection without consent
These collections have served as foundational resources for theory and methods development in biological anthropology into the 21st century
Workshop participants reached major points of consensus on ethical guidelines for research, teaching, community engagement, and curation

historical_significance.controversy_generated: Addresses ongoing tensions between scientific research interests and ethical obligations to descendant communities and marginalized populations
historical_significance.lasting_impact: Establishes framework for ethical reform in biological anthropology that could transform how historical human remains collections are studied, taught with, and curated

paper.title: Endogenous Colonial Borders: Precolonial States and Geography in the Partition of Africa
paper.authors: Jack Paine, Xiaoyan Qiu, Joan Ricart‐Huguet
paper.abstract_preview: We revise the conventional wisdom that Africa’s international borders were drawn arbitrarily. Europeans knew very little about most of Africa in the mid-1880s, but their self-interested goals of amass...

concepts.name: Endogenous Colonial Borders
concepts.definition: The concept that African colonial borders were not arbitrarily drawn but were influenced by precolonial political structures, geography, and European-African negotiations

concepts.name: African Agency in Border Formation
concepts.definition: The influence that African rulers and political entities exercised on colonial border demarcation through treaties and negotiations with Europeans

concepts.name: Precolonial State Frontiers
concepts.definition: Historical boundaries and territorial divisions of African political entities that existed before European colonization

concepts.name: Geographic Focal Points in Border Formation
concepts.definition: The role of natural features, particularly water bodies, as determining factors in colonial border placement

concepts.name: Causal Process Observations for Borders
concepts.definition: Systematic coding of historical evidence to identify causal mechanisms behind each bilateral border formation

relationships.explanation: Directly challenges the conventional wisdom that African borders were drawn arbitrarily by Europeans
Builds upon existing colonial studies by emphasizing African actors' influence in colonial processes

relationships.from: Geographic Focal Points in Border Formation
relationships.explanation: Applies geographic determinism concepts to explain colonial border placement patterns
Combines quantitative spatial analysis with qualitative historical evidence examination

claims.claim: Historical political frontiers directly affected 62% of all bilateral borders in Africa
claims.evidence_type: Quantitative analysis of original spatial dataset and qualitative coding of treaties

claims.claim: Europeans negotiated with African rulers to secure treaties and learn about historical state frontiers
Water bodies comprised the primary border feature much more frequently than straight lines
claims.evidence_type: Systematic analysis of border characteristics across all bilateral borders

claims.claim: Europeans' self-interested goals prompted intensive examination of on-the-ground conditions during border formation
claims.evidence_type: Historical diplomatic records and treaty negotiations

claims.claim: Major water bodies shaped precolonial civilizations and served as focal points for border formation
claims.evidence_type: Spatial analysis and historical evidence of precolonial states

historical_significance.controversy_generated: Challenges fundamental assumptions about colonial legacy and African state formation, potentially controversial among scholars who emphasize colonial arbitrariness
historical_significance.lasting_impact: Likely to reshape understanding of African political geography and colonial processes, with implications for debates about African state legitimacy and border conflicts

paper.title: Doing migration studies with an accent
paper.abstract_preview: I use accent as a metaphor and a conceptual tool to epistemologically reflect over the field of migration studies. Accented thinking is a response and a reaction to the condition of coloniality that s...

concepts.name: accented thinking
concepts.definition: An epistemological approach using accent as metaphor for alternative knowledge production in response to coloniality in migration studies

concepts.name: epistemic refusal
concepts.definition: A combination of accent as way of knowing and form of struggle that refuses dominant epistemologies

concepts.name: accent as refusal
concepts.definition: Using accent as tool to scrutinize epistemic injustices and create alternative ways of knowing

concepts.name: coloniality in knowledge production
concepts.definition: Colonial structures that shape and constrain knowledge production processes in migration studies

relationships.explanation: Accented thinking responds to coloniality and seeks alternative knowledge production, aligning with decolonial epistemological projects
Epistemic refusal extends concepts of epistemic disobedience by combining knowing and struggle through accent metaphor
Uses accent as tool to analyze and scrutinize epistemic injustices in migration studies
Challenges dominant approaches in migration studies by proposing alternative epistemological frameworks

claims.claim: Accented thinking can stimulate emergence of new perspectives and approaches in migration studies
Accent functions both as a way of knowing and a form of struggle
Coloniality structures knowledge production processes in migration studies
Accented thinking takes us beyond notions that restrict alternative orders

historical_significance.controversy_generated: Potential debate over methodological validity and practical application of accent metaphor in academic research
historical_significance.lasting_impact: Contributes to ongoing decolonial turn in migration studies by offering novel metaphorical framework for epistemological critique

paper.title: Refusal (and Repair)
paper.abstract_preview: This article focuses on the concept of refusal, particularly as it has been developed within critical Black studies and critical Indigenous studies within anthropology and beyond. It argues that while...

concepts.name: refusal
concepts.definition: A concept developed within critical Black studies and critical Indigenous studies that provides alternative approaches to understanding political struggle and resistance beyond traditional Western frameworks

concepts.name: afterlives of conquest
concepts.definition: The ongoing effects and continuing presence of colonial violence and dispossession that persist in contemporary political and social formations

concepts.name: coloniality
concepts.definition: The ongoing structural and epistemic dimensions of colonial power that continue to shape modern political life globally

concepts.name: modern personhood
concepts.definition: The foundational processes that established possibilities for political subjectivity and agency in modernity, particularly in Western contexts

relationships.explanation: Refusal builds upon but addresses limitations in Foucauldian frameworks by attending to foundational processes of modern personhood
Refusal extends Gramscian approaches to power by incorporating Black and Indigenous radical thought genealogies
The concept of refusal has been developed within critical Black studies as a framework for understanding political struggle
Refusal emerges from critical Indigenous studies as an approach to political resistance and thought

claims.claim: Foucauldian and Gramscian frameworks have not sufficiently attended to foundational processes that charted possibilities of modern personhood and political life globally
Traditional power analysis frameworks have not adequately acknowledged genealogies of Black and Indigenous radical thought informing political approaches in these communities
Any significant reformulation of anthropology must recognize and foreground ongoing coloniality and racism as afterlives of conquest
Refusal provides inroads to reformulating anthropological approaches to political struggle

historical_significance.controversy_generated: Challenges established theoretical frameworks in anthropology and calls for disciplinary reformulation
historical_significance.lasting_impact: Contributes to ongoing decolonial turn in anthropology and foregrounds Indigenous and Black radical thought as central to understanding political resistance
